title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
Welcome!
Another blog from a person interested in science. What is different here? Well, let’s see. I do not know yet myself. Maybe nothing except for the fact that written blogs run out of fashion anyways and my expectation is that nobody’s going to read it anyways. This reduces the pressure immensely. There is the old joke that one should store some money in ones thesis at the library. It’s a save place!

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
What can I exact?
I will write mostly about exercise and human movement sciences related to health. I also have a strong interest in psychology and statistics but that doesn’t mean I am an expert in any of those areas.
Some posts will be of practical importance and deal with physical exercise or athletic training. Others might be more theoretical or philosophical and might not give you the definitive answer you are looking for. In any case, I try to back up my ideas with scientific theories or direct findings. I will make sure to cite the origin of the knowledge-base I tap into as good as possible (until no original thought can be thought anymore).

title: Welcome To My Blog
section: Why do you write this blog?
text:
Why do you write this blog?
Pure scholarship. Allan Watts talks about the idea of studying as leisure and how scholarship contradicts itself without the playfulness invited (Allan Watts). It’s hard to grasp for me but I try to follow the principle here. The blog is a way to try ideas out. Let them sink. It’s low risk and low cost compared to writing a book or a paper but I still might get some feedback from outside. That might be the main idea (as far as I understand myself).
So, have fun reading!

title: Post With Code
text:
This is a post with executable code.

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: About
text:
Dear reader,
this blog is my effort to make sense of everything exercise science, human movement and psychology. There is no direct purpose behind this blog. It is said that many great scientist answered in a similar way when asked about the practical implications of their findings:
We don’t do it because it is important - We do it because it is interesting.
This blog is mainly a personal experiment. (No, I do not need a hypothesis for that.) Feel free to leave comments or send me a message. No likes or subscriptions are needed. Only Buy Me A Coffee when we meet in person.

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
Honestly, I do not know yet! Maybe nothing, except that written blogs run out of fashion. My expectation is that nobody will read it. This reduces the pressure immensely! The old joke is that if you are looking for a safe place to store money, out it in your thesis at the library. It will be there in times of need.
I hope to store some thoughts here and discover what happens to them if exposed to the virtual world. Maybe an AI system picks them up and my mind will be reincarnated in a couple of decades. Maybe the texts reach some other biological minds leading to interesting conversations and discussions. Maybe this blog just stays there for a couple of months and vanishes vastness of cyberspace. I do not feel confident by any of those predictions. But I am sure my future self will find a nice story about that blog and why it turned out the way it did…

title: Welcome To My Blog
section: Why do you write this blog?
text:
Why do you write this blog?
Pure scholarship! Allan Watts talked about studying as leisure and how scholarship contradicts itself when playfulness is not invited (Allan Watts). It’s hard to grasp, but I am trying to follow this principle. The blog is a way to try out ideas - let them sink. It’s low risk and cost compared to writing a book or a paper. But I still might get some feedback from outside. That might be the main idea (as far as I understand).
So, I wish myself fun, and I am happy to share it :-)

title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
text:
The first scientific journal was installed by the Royal Society in 1665, and today, scientific publications in peer-reviewed journals are the most important currency in science. I guess it is with good reason. Publishers made it possible for the community to distribute the knowledge (rather slowly, one would argue). It is conceivable that our research progress around the world would have been way slower without those printed journals.
Today the big publishers get a lot of bad press. The scientific community seems to be especially frustrated with the current situation. Not a week goes in which I do not hear a colleague complaining or making self-aware jokes about the publishing system and its effects on academia. But in this post, I do not want to go into that in more detail. There is a lot of discussion about it, and everybody seems to know what Elsevier, Springer, and Whiley should do to make the world a better place. It is a bit like during the soccer world-cup during which everyone becomes a sports analyst and coach.
Here I want to approach the problem from another perspective. What can scientists do to improve the situation?
Maybe not much, because your publication list is what makes you a successful scientist. Publish or perish! First of all, the truth of this motto is jet to be determined. As the next generation of scientists climbs the academic ladder, they increasingly understand that journal publications are just one of the things that make good researchers. At one point, political decision-makers and funding agencies will understand too. But the first steps have to be done from the ground up.

text:

But in this post, I do not want to go into that in more detail. There is a lot of discussion about it, and everybody seems to know what Elsevier, Springer, and Whiley should do to make the world a better place. Here I want to approach the problem from another perspective. What can scientists do to improve the situation?
Maybe not much, because your publication list is what makes you a successful scientist. Publish or perish! First of all, the truth of this motto is jet to be determined. title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
section: Double Committement - Life Outside the Ivory Tower
text:
Double Committement - Life Outside the Ivory Tower
As I have written on the front page, science, and scholarship are activities of leisure. But scientists have to live on something too. Traditionally, many Western scientists have lived from family wealth. Many did the experiments and observations out of curiosity, leisure or to reach some kind of fame. Nowadays things are different, obviously. While academic researchers do enjoy prestige in some regards, the money that can be made is relatively limited. This is especially true concerning extensive education, high working hours, and the low probability of reaching the top of the food chain, which mostly means getting tenure. An alternative route for many is going into industry. This is a more realistic route for some research areas than others and nearly impossible for some.



academia vs industry


Sometimes, there is some middle ground. Some scientists hit the “jackpot” and to both. They enjoy the freedom of academia and, as a related side-show, they work in the private sector. For health and performance-related fields (the ones that I am most interested in) like sport- and exercise science, psychology, and nutrition, this might mean giving seminars, selling books, working with supplement companies, or leveraging one’s research idea into profitable products.
I can tell you that this is huge in this sector, and probably some big names come to your mind that do exactly that. So, in essence, they are selling the expertise they have carved out over years or decades of work. Fair thing, right? Let me make this clear: Some of independent scientists directly use the leverage they gain to fund their research with good intentions. Some claim that their breakthroughs would have not been possible in the traditional way because nobody beliefed in their work and they couldn’t get funding.
But researchers are faulty human beings. No matter if they are funded by a national funding agency, by their personal company, or by personal beliefs and the urge to be right. We are invested in our ideas and hypothesis. I do not know if everybody iss, but I certainly am1. I do not believe that being invested in an idea is completely avoidable. But caution is advisable if scientists sell products (or knowledge) related to their research without doing that directly as or in their role of the research institution. It might sound like a good thing if popular Stanford professors state that their podcast is independent of their role as a researcher, but I am not convinced that can be true. Not only because they (probably) have no chance but being the same person!
Sure, “research is me-search” - and personal investment might come with higher motivation, drive and creativity. This makes the objective work vulnerable and contaminates your role as a researcher. Another profession knows about that for hundreds and thousands of years. So, in many traditions, priests are not allowed to have a family or sex. That’s how they stay independent and hard to bribe (in theory).

title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
section: But Why is Selling Books Bad?
text:
But Why is Selling Books Bad?
Making research available to the public is a significant component of modern science and is incentivized and with good reason. We want educated and responsible citizens!
BUT WHY SELL IT??? That’s the whole point.
We, want people - all people - to get access to the knowledge base. We are unhappy with the power of big publishing houses. But, it is hard to change that in the case of peer-reviewed articles without risking one’s career. We talk about the FAIR principles within the ivory tower of science. But why not expand these principles beyond that?
We can and should leverage all those problems into unrestricted publications via blogs or free internet books.
Publish your teaching openly: I myself started putting one of my undergraduate seminars into writing and publishing it on Bookdown2. This makes my seminar not only more transparent for students but also allows the information to be updated on a regular basis. Even after finishing the course, students can easily access the information. One could argue that you no longer need to go to the seminar as all the information is accessible for free on the Internet. But in my opinion, that is the case anyway. I can not tell our students any exercise training secrets anyway. Nobody should go to university for that explicit, teachable information anymore. We have passed that point a long time ago! The good news is that we can focus on experimental learning and carve out competencies3!
Writing blog posts about your research: Traditional scientific journal articles can only withstand so much information. The general consensus in many fields is not to spoil the reader with too much personal experience, different challenges during different phases of the project. I think even in pre-prints and other publishing formats that are more researcher centered, we hold on to the traditional writing style like using passive voice as if everything was executed by a emotionless and error-free research-robot. In reality there is often some kind of thinking, tweaking, rethinking and frustration involved. Sharing those things might improve project science. Writing those protocols in a personal way might not only help yourself resolving some of the project related issues but also make the work of the grad-student working on a similar project at another university easier. Humanizing our daily work by writing about it, is also a way to make science and scientists more relate-able for people outside of academia. We do have this problem of trust towards science and just informing people “how reality works” does not do the trick to improve that, in my opinion. But being relate-able is also something we should work for within Team Science. Seeing that all science (not only my project) is messy. There are struggles that go beyond low statistical power and deadlines. I personally would love to see more unrestricted, writing from scientists. I would love to see less profit oriented side-gigs and more real content - raw, personal and messy as it is and I hope you do too. Because that is what I will be trying with this blog.

title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
text:
Footnotes


and if there is one thing I learned from stats, it’s that, most likely, I am a typical person and others think and act similarly.↩︎

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
What can you expect?
I will mostly write about the sciences of exercise and human movement related to health. I also have a strong interest in psychology and statistics but that doesn’t mean I am an expert in any of those areas.
Some posts will be of practical nature and deal with physical exercise or athletic training. Others might be more theoretical or philosophical at times. In any case, I will try to back up my ideas with scientific theories and my readings of the current literature. I will do as I was taught and cite the “original” thought as good as possible (until no original thought can be thought anymore).

title: Welcome To My Blog
section: Why do I write this blog?
text:
Why do I write this blog?
Pure scholarship! Allan Watts was talking about studying as leisure and how scholarship contradicts itself when playfulness is not invited (Allan Watts). It’s hard to grasp, but I am trying to follow this principle. The blog is a way to try out ideas - let them express themselfs and then rest and sink[^1]. It seems like low risk and low cost compared to writing a book or a paper. Not writing (or not making to public) would rob me of the full experience. The intention to publish it makes one think harder and care about the writings.
So, I wish myself fun, and I am happy to share it
[^1]: Yes, I might think of ideas as entities sometimes.

title: How should we address Stress in Rehabilitation and Health care
text:
This paper first discusses the concept of stress and its effect on health. After defining stress as we use it in this paper, we discuss the effects of stress on health relevant metrics. We then provide a framework for healthcare professionals to adress stress in patients and clients. At the end, we provide direct strategies to implement in a preventative and rehabilitative context.
Stress is an interactive process between the environmental demands, the capacity of the organism handling them and the psychobiological response given. Stress most likely emerges if the situation corresponds to a potential, imagend or actual psychological, social, or physical threat to the integrity of the person.
The described interplay between demands, capacity, and response is hard to measure directly. Therefore, stress is operationalized mainly based on the stress response component of the process. The human stress responses can be categorized into behavioral, cognitive-emotional and physiological effects. The hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and the sympathetic nervous system (SNS) are core to the stress responses. Frequently described mediators are ACTH and cortisol for the HPA axis, as well as adrenaline, nor-adrenaline, heart rate, heart rate variability, blood pressure and alpha-amylase for the SNS.
Mechanistically, the main driver for adverse health outcomes is chronic dysregulation of cortisol, which affects nearly every cell of the human body. The interaction of cortisol with the immune system creates downstream effects on wound healing, and cardiovascular, metabolic, neurological, and psychological health.
As health care professionals (HCP) we should think of stress as a potential long-term threat to health comparable to high blood pressure. Chronic stress also impacts health behavior and adherence to treatment plans. Stress interventions should incorporate all levels of the discussed models: The demand, the capacity and the response (behavioral, emotional-cognitive and physiological) and should be executed under the umbrella of client-centered care. Lastly, we discuss strategies that can be implemented in rehabilitative and preventetive settings.



What is stress?
Since the early days of stress research, there was a debate about what stress is. Past research focused on the stress response as an unspecific reaction to environmental stressors. Hans Selye defined this as the General Adaptation Syndrom (selyeGENERALADAPTATIONSYNDROME1946?). In this school of thought, stress was the biological endresult, that lead to multiple negative health outcomes like catabolism, hypoglycemia or ulcrea (selyeStressGeneralAdaptation1950?; selyeStressDistress1975?).
On the other hand, some research focuses on the demand to the organism. This is evident in the use of “oxidative stress” or “heat stress” in mostly articles in physiology or related areas (teleanuOverviewOxidativeStress2022?; periardExerciseHeatStress2021?). As a third definition, stress is sometimes described defined as an interactive process between the envirnomental demand and the organisms reaction, mediated by psychological processes. This view was heavily influenced by (lazarusCognitiveTheoriesStress1986?) and (masonHistoricalViewStress1975?). Other definitions are surely possible. For example, in their book, (everlyClinicalGuideTreatment2013?), define stress as „[…] a physiological response that serves as a mechanism of mediation linking any given stressor to its target-organ effect.“ (Everly, and Lating, 2013, p. 17). This is also a response oriented definition but sees the stress response as a general neuroendocinological messenger system. This definition therefore also serves as a representative example of “stress as function”. Each of these definitions come with some weaknesses that have already been explored by (masonHistoricalViewStress1975?). Little has changed in the last 5 decades and we still hold to his recommendtation to state the definition that is used in the present work.
In the present article, we use the word stress for the link function between the demand, the capacity and the physiological response. The demand can be any environmetal challange to the hoemostasis. This challange can be real, imangend or predicted and are compared to the attributed ressources and hinderences. We propose a framework of baysian updating Steffen for this interplay. The brain, as the center of the stress regulation (mcewenBrainStressIntegrative2013?) predictes costs and benefits of the response. But from the stance of evolutionary biology, the smoke-detector principle Nesse, 2005…
Stress was defined in a variety of ways by different researchers. The model by Lazarus and Folkman (SOURCE) provides the foundation of this article. Based on this model, we introduce stress as stress as an interactive process between stressor, as the demands on the organism, and the final response. The final response can be any form of coping attempt. The physiological stress response was first described by Cannon and later taken up by Seyle. Accordingly, we define the physiological stress response as general responses by the ANS and HPA-axis, sending preparatory signals to the end organs.
Lazarus and Folkman described primary and secondary appraisal as the mediating force between the stessor, response and capacity.
How can we measure stress?

(lazarusCognitiveTheoriesStress1986?) realized that describing stress only by one component (mostly by stress response (Seyle) or stressor (WHO?) leads to circular arguments and limit scientific progress. Models that focus on the process are able ot detect and describe effects form multiple perspectives.
What makes stress stressful




Stress on the immune system (might be the overarching topic)
Stress on cardiovascular system
Stress on metabolic diseases and obesity
Stress on wound-healing
Stress and Psychopathologies (Depression, Anxiety, PTSD, burn-out, …)





Working on the stressor
Optimizing the stress response mediators (HRV, Cortisol, …)
Mental coping mechanisms (reframeing, mindfulness, mentalization of strong emotions, …)
Working on the long-term outcomes of health





People do not like to go to the hcp, why?

They might not feel heared
They might feel guilty or shame (have not dealt with it earlier, body image, …)
They might not like the effort
They might be afraid of acute pain
(of coures there are aother rasons like mistrust in health care or lack of ressources)





Stress is as a frame or concept that is talked about a lot. People use the word to describe their experiance of being overwelmed, out of sync with their needs and desires or struggle to find time time for things that are important to them or are in line with the personality theiy want to be.
Stress can be differentiated based on the nature of the stressor as daily hussels and stressful life events. The daily hussels is what most people use the wording “I am stressed” for. This kind of stress can be described as a stait of mind. Describing stress as being in onse head (or mind) does not mean that it is not real. As Descarde described it, there is just one thing we can be certain of being real and that is consciousness. This is partly represented in his most famous line: “cognito ergo sum”. Stress being a stait of mind means that the experiance of stress can not be fully predicted by environmental variables. The feeling we most likely experiance in the stait of stress are one of being out of sync with onse desires and needs. We want to take more time off and relax but we can not find the time to do so. The feeling of stress can also represent itself as helplessness or being overwhelmed, which is often combined with uncontrolability and uncertainty. Again, it is not important if an external observer- you the therapist - thinks about the external validity of that description. It is their reality and there is little chance a short explanation of someone that does not stand in their shoes can change that feeling.
So what is thee better approach? First we need to make sure people feel heard and cared for. Your listening skills play a crucial part in this game. Nevertheless, I want to briefly point out my personal shortcoming in this regards. Working with clients and patients I was so obsessed with this idea, that sometimes I pushed back even when someone was ready for a solution. Especially for those hcp that are not trained as a psychologiest or psychotherapist, yes listen but remember that you do not have the toolbox to solve the problem this way.
So let the actions talk for you. Check in with them and see if they are ready, willing and able to create new experiences. You can ask them if they are ready to change some of their behaviour or their daily structure. Yes we have to work with the patient and it is good that we do not order anymore. On the other hand, if they are ready, willing and able to do so, give them clear and concsice instructions and frame it as an experiment. Don’t forget that an experiment has to test and therefor measure something. If the outcome is concerning subjective experience, this is the measurement tool we have to be able to trust.
Every digital kitchen scale starts with the reading of TARE or some other symbole that indicates that it is calibrating accordingly. When you use other measuring devices in your clinical practice you most likely do something similar or you trust in the built in calibration. Similarly our patients (and we) have to calibrate our detectors of feelings and emotions 1. You might also think of it as ankering, even if I think this term is not sufficiant. The calibration in this case takes time and is nothing we can push. During this process patients learn to differentiate between different levels of stress and relaxation. So we have to first at least invite both staits. Most likely we can create a frame for relaxation in our practice. Most of the existing literature would then advice to make this experiance explicite; meaning that patient should describe this state most likely via describing bodily sensations. This approach might work for many people. But i am sceptical as this approach relys on the linguistic abilities of the person. Also it takes for granted that language based categorising has some special effect compared to other non-language based categorisations.

This kind of stress can be described as a stait of mind. Describing stress as being in onse head (or mind) does not mean that it is not real. As Descarde described it, there is just one thing we can be certain of being real and that is consciousness. This is partly represented in his most famous line: “cognito ergo sum”. Stress being a stait of mind means that the experiance of stress can not be fully predicted by environmental variables. The feeling we most likely experiance in the stait of stress are one of being out of sync with onse desires and needs. We want to take more time off and relax but we can not find the time to do so. The feeling of stress can also represent itself as helplessness or being overwhelmed, which is often combined with uncontrolability and uncertainty. Again, it is not important if an external observer- you the therapist - thinks about the external validity of that description. It is their reality and there is little chance a short explanation of someone that does not stand in their shoes can change that feeling.
So what is thee better approach? First we need to make sure people feel heard and cared for. Your listening skills play a crucial part in this game. Nevertheless, I want to briefly point out my personal shortcoming in this regards. Working with clients and patients I was so obsessed with this idea, that sometimes I pushed back even when someone was ready for a solution. Especially for those hcp that are not trained as a psychologiest or psychotherapist, yes listen but remember that you do not have the toolbox to solve the problem this way.
So let the actions talk for you. Check in with them and see if they are ready, willing and able to create new experiences. You can ask them if they are ready to change some of their behaviour or their daily structure. Yes we have to work with the patient and it is good that we do not order anymore. On the other hand, if they are ready, willing and able to do so, give them clear and concsice instructions and frame it as an experiment. Don’t forget that an experiment has to test and therefor measure something. If the outcome is concerning subjective experience, this is the measurement tool we have to be able to trust.
Every digital kitchen scale starts with the reading of TARE or some other symbole that indicates that it is calibrating accordingly. When you use other measuring devices in your clinical practice you most likely do something similar or you trust in the built in calibration. Similarly our patients (and we) have to calibrate our detectors of feelings and emotions 1. You might also think of it as ankering, even if I think this term is not sufficiant. The calibration in this case takes time and is nothing we can push. During this process patients learn to differentiate between different levels of stress and relaxation. So we have to first at least invite both staits. Most likely we can create a frame for relaxation in our practice. Most of the existing literature would then advice to make this experiance explicite; meaning that patient should describe this state most likely via describing bodily sensations. This approach might work for many people. But i am sceptical as this approach relys on the linguistic abilities of the person. Also it takes for granted that language based categorising has some special effect compared to other non-language based categorisations.

title: How should we address Stress in Rehabilitation and Health care
text:
Footnotes


I want ot stress that not we are calibrating our patients but they are calibrating their sensory experienace, feelings and emotions.↩︎
